Saving the number of trained samples in the model and using it with `len(train_set)` to calculate a start epoch breaks resuming with a different train set. Say you train for 10 epochs with a train set of 10 files. At the end of training, `n_samples_trained_on_model` = 100. Now you want to train for another 3 epochs on a set of 51 files. `n_samples_trained_on_model // len(train_set)` is 1, so it'll think it's starting from epoch 2, rather than epoch 11. `n_samples_already_trained` will be `100 % 51 = 49`, so it'll also skip 49 samples in the first epoch with the new data.

I think we should just use the epoch history returned by the checkpoint manager, and start from epoch `max(epoch_number, epoch_history) + 1`. Then the epoch count will be monotonically increasing, regardless of how many times you stopped and resumed, or if you changed the train set or not.

I had a whole thing written how it happened, because the stream can define its own symbols in a simple prefix tree.
When I now went back to the spec, I saw that it actually explicitly mentions:
> "If the integer value [for a symbol]
>   is greater than or equal to the alphabet size, then the stream
>   should be rejected as invalid."
I have missed that in my implementation thus far, and therefore it has been possible for a corrupt stream to create block type codes in a simple prefix code that exceed NBLTYPEx + 2.

I agree now that my suggested change is not needed. I will submit an amended change that fixes the type NBLTYPES -> NBLTYPESL.

An observation, the code example above will not work since Bigtable `Instance` resource only supports predefined roles: `'roles/bigtable.admin'`, `'roles/bigtable.reader'`, `'roles/bigtable.user'` and `'roles/bigtable.viewer'`.
The correct way would be 
```python
policy['roles/bigtable.admin'] = [
    policy.user("test_iam@test.com"),
    policy.service_account("sv_account@gmail.com")]
result = instance.set_iam_policy(policy)
print (result['roles/bigtable.admin'])
```
To enhance user's experience I am suggesting to add `policy.py` (gist [link](https://gist.github.com/AVaksman/66d1219a338f73dcf02cfb2b6bb2effa)) that extends `iam.Policy` to this library and reroute `set_iam_policy` method through it.
Then the workflow will change to 
```python
from google.cloud.bigtable.client import Client
from google.cloud.bigtable.policy import BIGTABLE_ADMIN_ROLE
from google.cloud.bigtable.policy import Policy

    client = Client(admin=True)
    instance = client.instance('[INSTANCE_ID]')
    policy = Policy()
    policy[BIGTABLE_ADMIN_ROLE] = [
        policy.user("test_iam@test.com"),
        policy.service_account("sv_account@gmail.com"),
    ]
    result = instance.set_iam_policy(policy)
    print (result.bigtable_admins)
```

I think that a method which requires an iterable of field names is [easier to comprehend](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/writing/style/#explicit-code) and will lead to generally nicer client code. What do I mean by "nicer client code?" Consider the case where I want to update all of an organization's fields except it's name. You could do so like this:

```python
org = …  # org is an Organization object.
org = org.update(set(org.get_values().keys()) - set(['name']))
```

If `*args` were required, the same code would look like this:

```python
org = …  # org is an Organization object.
org = org.update(*(set(org.get_values().keys()) - set(['name'])))
```

So we substitute the string literal `"{foo_name}"`... this works except somehow if the literal doesn't contain the usual "uuid:" qualifier, Flask decides to escape the curly braces.

In theory, I can separate it out. But while I am doing this work, I already noticed that there are 2 new Livy state that is not in the list. If I don't add the new states, the test could randomly fail. Those 2 states should not affect how sparkmagic works at all. 
I agree sparkmagic is depending on Livy and should have enough understanding of it. We need to know the ending states: Dead/Success and some key states like Idle. However, it shouldn't have a hard coded list of all Livy states and strictly enforcing them, especially these states are not used by magic anywhere else. Livy could add a new transition state any time, and I don't think we should put magic into a corner where a new update is needed for every such Livy change. I'm not familiar with `this._setFoo('')`?  The other two are different in that `this.set()` works for subproperties, there's some discussion about it here:
https://www.polymer-project.org/1.0/docs/devguide/data-system#observable-changes
https://www.polymer-project.org/1.0/docs/devguide/model-data#set-path

If you're assigning the property to a new object and aren't using subproperties then I think `this.set('foo', '')` and `this.foo = ''` are pretty much equivalent.  Note though that `this.set()` doesn't work for arrays and cases where it isn't a new object.  So it seems safest IMO to prefer direct assignment (which I think always works?) and only use the special polymer mutation methods when it's important to be more efficient by not discarding the existing object or array.

Yes.  Default page size is 1000.  Attempting to get file info on 1000 S3 objects from the Upload Service lambda took a considerable amount of time and was timing out at the API gateway (30s) and sometimes the Lambda timeout too (300s).  It also led to very log pauses in what should be a moderately interactive command.  Reducing to 100 keeps it within the API gateway timeout and makes the command feel more interactive.

I considered getting 1000 at a time from S3 then looping through that 100 at a time to get file info from the Upload Service but frankly I don't think that optimization is worth the extra code complexity.  99% of the work is talking to the Upload Service anyway.
